description: Sarraceniaceae contains three genera of pitcher plants native to the Americas: Sarracenia (North American pitcher plants), Darlingtonia (cobra lily), and Heliamphora (sun pitchers of South America). All trap insects in hollow pitcher-shaped leaves. Most are beginner-friendly and hardy in temperate gardens.
meta_description: Shop Sarracenia, Darlingtonia, and Heliamphora. Hardy North and South American pitcher plants with striking colouration. Beginner to expert species available.
meta_keywords: Sarracenia, Darlingtonia, Heliamphora, North American pitcher plant, carnivorous plant

genera.description: Sarracenia comprises 8–11 species native to eastern North America, from Florida to Canada. Their upright tubular pitchers are among the most ornamental of all carnivorous plants. Hardy and beginner-friendly, most tolerate outdoor bog gardens in temperate climates and require a cold winter dormancy.
genera.meta_description: Buy Sarracenia pitcher plants. Hardy, colourful, and easy to grow in outdoor bog gardens. Species, hybrids, and named cultivars available as TC-grown plants.
genera.meta_keywords: Sarracenia, North American pitcher plant, bog garden plant, hardy carnivorous plant, Sarracenia flava, Sarracenia purpurea
genera.native_range: Eastern North America — from Florida panhandle north to Nova Scotia and west to Mississippi

species.description: Sarracenia purpurea is the most widespread Sarracenia, ranging from Florida to Labrador. Its squat, fat pitchers rely on rainwater and rely on bacteria rather than enzymes to digest prey. Two subspecies: ssp. purpurea (northern; green with red veining) and ssp. venosa (southern; more heavily veined). Extremely cold-hardy — survives frozen solid.
species.meta_description: Hardy Sarracenia purpurea — most cold-tolerant pitcher plant. Survives freezing. Ideal for UK and northern European bog gardens. Multiple cultivars.
species.meta_keywords: Sarracenia purpurea, purple pitcher plant, hardy bog plant, cold hardy carnivorous plant
species.native_range: Eastern North America — Florida to Labrador (bogs, fens, wet heathland)
species.notes: Extremely cold hardy. Does not need overhead protection in UK winters. Divide clumps every 3-4 years.

cultivars.description: Robust southern form with vivid red veining; selected from Chipola River population, Florida.
Compact form of ssp. venosa var. burkii; pitcher mouths heavily lidded; deep burgundy colouration.

species.description: Sarracenia flava produces tall, elegant yellow-green pitchers up to 90 cm. The lid holds the pitcher open. Native to the south-eastern USA, it is highly variable with numerous colour forms — from pure yellow-green to deep red. One of the most spectacular and widely grown Sarracenia.
species.meta_title: Sarracenia flava — Yellow Pitcher Plant Cultivars
species.meta_description: Tall, elegant Sarracenia flava in multiple colour forms. Copper Top, Maxima, Ornata — TC-grown cultivars for bog gardens and greenhouse collections.
species.meta_keywords: Sarracenia flava, yellow pitcher plant, Sarracenia Copper Top, tall pitcher plant
species.native_range: South-eastern USA — Virginia to Florida panhandle (wet pine savannas)
species.notes: Requires winter dormancy. Tall pitchers need protection from strong wind. Full sun essential for best colouration.

cultivars.description: All-green form with no red pigmentation; pure yellow-green pitchers and yellow flower.
Red lid only, green tube — creates a striking copper-topped appearance in full sun.
Heavy red veining covering most of the pitcher surface; one of the most ornate colour forms.

species.description: Sarracenia leucophylla is arguably the most beautiful Sarracenia. The upper portion of the pitcher and lid are white with green and red venation, creating a stained-glass effect. Vulnerable due to habitat loss in Gulf Coast pine savannas. Autumn-flushing — produces its best pitchers in late summer and autumn.
species.meta_description: Stunning Sarracenia leucophylla with white-top pitchers and intricate red veining. Vulnerable in the wild. TC-grown plants with provenance documentation.
species.meta_keywords: Sarracenia leucophylla, white pitcher plant, Tarnok Sarracenia, hurricane creek white
species.native_range: USA — Gulf Coast (Florida panhandle, Alabama, Georgia, Mississippi)
species.notes: Autumn-flushing species — produces its most spectacular pitchers Aug-Oct. Cold-hardy despite southern origin.

cultivars.description: Famous double-flowered form; flowers are sterile rosettes; one of the most distinctive Sarracenia cultivars.
Ghostly white form with minimal veining; almost entirely white pitchers — extremely striking.
Pristine white-topped pitchers with very fine red veining; selected from Hurricane Creek, Alabama population.

species.description: Sarracenia rubra is a complex of closely related forms producing narrow, dark-veined pitchers and fragrant dark red flowers with a sweet scent. Several subspecies are recognised across the south-eastern USA coastal plain. Near Threatened due to habitat fragmentation.
species.meta_description: Fragrant-flowered Sarracenia rubra with narrow, dark-veined pitchers. Multiple subspecies available. Near Threatened — TC provenance important.
species.meta_keywords: Sarracenia rubra, sweet pitcher plant, fragrant Sarracenia
species.native_range: South-eastern USA — North Carolina south to Georgia and Alabama (seepage bogs)
species.notes: Compact growing; good for smaller bog containers. Dark red flowers are sweetly fragrant — unusual for Sarracenia.

species.description: Sarracenia alata produces tall, pale yellow-green pitchers with a hood held nearly erect. Native to the Gulf Coast region; closely related to S. flava but with narrower pitchers and distinct genetics. Highly variable — some populations show deep red colouration.
species.meta_description: Tall, elegant Sarracenia alata from Gulf Coast pine savannas. Yellow-green to red forms. Easy, cold-hardy bog garden pitcher plant.
species.meta_keywords: Sarracenia alata, pale pitcher plant, Gulf Coast Sarracenia
species.native_range: USA — Louisiana, Mississippi, Alabama, Texas (wet pine savannas)
species.notes: Similar culture to S. flava. Some red-lidded forms very ornamental. Full sun, poor acidic soil.

species.description: Sarracenia psittacina is unique among Sarracenia for its lobster-pot trap design — the hooded, curved pitchers trap insects (and occasionally small aquatic animals) that cannot back out. The plant grows prostrate, often in seasonally flooded habitats. Highly ornamental.
species.meta_description: Unique prostrate Sarracenia psittacina with lobster-pot pitchers. Vivid red and white colouration. Tolerates flooding. Hardy carnivorous plant.
species.meta_keywords: Sarracenia psittacina, parrot pitcher plant, prostrate Sarracenia
species.native_range: USA — Florida, Georgia, Mississippi, Louisiana (seasonally flooded pine flatwoods)
species.notes: Prostrate growth; tolerates seasonal flooding. Red and white patterned pitchers very ornamental. Full sun.

species.description: Sarracenia minor has distinctive windows (areoles) — translucent patches near the top of the pitcher — that confuse trapped insects attempting to escape. The hood curves over the pitcher mouth. Native to the south-eastern USA.
species.meta_description: Sarracenia minor with distinctive translucent window patches. Compact, easy, ornamental. Ideal for bog garden collections.
species.meta_keywords: Sarracenia minor, hooded pitcher plant, window pitcher plant
species.native_range: USA — North Carolina south through Florida (wet pine flatwoods)
species.notes: Translucent areoles unique to this species. Compact growth. Full sun, standard Sarracenia care.

species.description: Sarracenia oreophila is the rarest and most endangered Sarracenia, restricted to a handful of sites in north-eastern Alabama and adjacent Georgia. Produces tall green pitchers with a hooded lid in spring, switching to flat phyllodia in summer. Federally Endangered in the USA. All cultivated plants must have legitimate TC provenance.
species.meta_title: Sarracenia oreophila — Endangered Green Pitcher Plant
species.meta_description: Federally Endangered Sarracenia oreophila — Green Pitcher Plant. Rare Alabama endemic. TC-grown plants only with full conservation provenance.
species.meta_keywords: Sarracenia oreophila, green pitcher plant, endangered Sarracenia, rare carnivorous plant
species.native_range: USA — north-eastern Alabama and adjacent Georgia (montane seepage bogs, 200-600 m)
species.notes: Federally Endangered (USA). TC provenance essential — wild collection is a federal crime. Produces non-carnivorous phyllodia in summer. Hardy.

genera.description: Darlingtonia californica is the sole species in its genus — a striking pitcher plant from cold mountain seeps in Oregon and northern California. The hooded pitcher resembles a cobra ready to strike, complete with a forked tongue-like appendage beneath the hood. It requires cold, constantly moving water at its roots and is the most challenging commonly grown Sarraceniaceae.
genera.meta_description: Spectacular Darlingtonia californica — the Cobra Lily. Cold-water seep specialist from Oregon and California. Intermediate-expert carnivorous plant.
genera.meta_keywords: Darlingtonia californica, cobra lily, cobra plant, California pitcher plant
genera.native_range: USA — Klamath Mountains, Oregon and northern California (cold mountain seeps)

species.description: Darlingtonia californica grows in ice-cold, fast-moving, nutrient-free mountain seep water. In cultivation it requires cool root temperatures — most growers use flowing cold water or ice packs in summer. The twisted, hooded pitchers are among the most architecturally dramatic of all carnivorous plants.
species.meta_title: Darlingtonia californica — Cobra Lily for Sale
species.meta_description: Buy Darlingtonia californica cobra lily. Cold-root conditions required. TC-grown plants. One of the most dramatic-looking carnivorous plants available.
species.meta_keywords: Darlingtonia californica, cobra lily, buy cobra plant, cold pitcher plant
species.native_range: USA — Klamath Mountains (Oregon/California border), mountain seeps and streambanks
species.notes: Roots must stay cold (below 15°C in summer). Use cool, flowing, mineral-free water. Full sun. Stolon offsets produced freely once established.

cultivars.description: Select form with deep red-purple pitchers; very ornamental in high light.
Vigorous clone from Mt Shasta provenance; large pitchers; reliable performer in cultivation.

genera.description: Heliamphora comprises approximately 23 species from the ancient tepui table-mountains of Venezuela, Guyana, and Brazil. Their simple, tube-like pitchers have a small nectar spoon on the back rather than a proper lid. They grow at high altitude in cool, constantly moist cloud-forest conditions — making them challenging but extraordinarily rewarding to cultivate.
genera.meta_title: Heliamphora — Sun Pitchers from the Tepuis
genera.meta_description: Buy Heliamphora sun pitchers from Venezuela's tepui mountains. Cool highland conditions. TC-grown plants for advanced carnivorous plant collectors.
genera.meta_keywords: Heliamphora, sun pitcher, tepui pitcher plant, Venezuelan carnivorous plant
genera.native_range: Venezuela, Guyana, Brazil — Guiana Highlands tepui table-mountains (1,000-2,800 m)

species.description: Heliamphora nutans is the most widely cultivated Heliamphora, native to Roraima-tepui. Its narrow, elegant pitchers nod at the tip and it is more tolerant of cultivation conditions than most species. A good entry point to the genus.
species.meta_description: Most beginner-friendly Heliamphora. Heliamphora nutans from Roraima-tepui — elegant nodding pitchers. Cool conditions required.
species.meta_keywords: Heliamphora nutans, sun pitcher, Roraima tepui plant
species.notes: Most tolerant Heliamphora for cultivation. Cool days (18-24°C), cool nights (8-15°C). High humidity. Pure water only.

cultivars.description: Form from Akopan-tepui; larger pitchers with pronounced nectar spoon.

species.description: Heliamphora minor is a compact species from Auyan-tepui. Despite its small stature, it produces very attractive pitchers and is one of the more reliable Heliamphora in cultivation. Notable for producing orange-red flowers.
species.meta_description: Compact Heliamphora minor from Auyan-tepui. Orange-red flowers, manageable size. TC-grown plants for tepui plant collectors.
species.meta_keywords: Heliamphora minor, small sun pitcher, Auyan-tepui plant
species.notes: Compact; suitable for smaller growing spaces than most Heliamphora. Orange-red flowers attractive.

cultivars.description: Select clone with especially vivid orange-red flower colouration and robust growth.

species.description: Heliamphora heterodoxa grows on the Gran Sabana plateau and several tepuis in Venezuela. It produces robust, wide pitchers and is one of the more reliable species in cultivation. A good second Heliamphora after H. nutans.
species.meta_title: Heliamphora heterodoxa — Gran Sabana Sun Pitcher
species.meta_description: Robust Heliamphora heterodoxa from Venezuela's Gran Sabana. Wide pitchers, reliable in cultivation. Good intermediate Heliamphora.
species.meta_keywords: Heliamphora heterodoxa, Gran Sabana pitcher, Venezuelan sun pitcher
species.native_range: Venezuela — Gran Sabana, Ptari-tepui, Soro-muru-tepui (1,200-2,200 m)
species.notes: One of the more robust species. Slightly warmer tolerances than ultra-highland species. Good for intermediate Heliamphora growers.

species.description: Heliamphora tatei is one of the largest Heliamphora, producing massive pitchers from the remote Cerro de la Neblina on the Brazil-Venezuela border. It is among the most sought-after and challenging tepui plants in cultivation. Named after the botanist George Tate.
species.meta_description: Massive Heliamphora tatei from Cerro de la Neblina. One of the largest and most sought-after sun pitchers. For advanced tepui plant collectors.
species.meta_keywords: Heliamphora tatei, giant sun pitcher, Neblina tepui plant, large Heliamphora
species.native_range: Brazil/Venezuela border — Cerro de la Neblina (1,800-3,000 m)
species.notes: Large and spectacular but demanding. Very cool, humid conditions essential. Advanced growers only.

species.description: Heliamphora ionasii produces some of the most ornate pitchers in the genus, with a large, elaborate nectar spoon and striking red colouration. Endemic to Ilu-tepui and Tramen-tepui in Venezuela. Endangered due to its extremely restricted range.
species.meta_title: Heliamphora ionasii — Ornate Endangered Sun Pitcher
species.meta_description: Spectacular Heliamphora ionasii with elaborate nectar spoon and red colouration. Endangered. TC-grown plants only. Expert care conditions.
species.meta_keywords: Heliamphora ionasii, ornate sun pitcher, endangered Heliamphora, Ilu-tepui
species.notes: Very restricted wild range. Elaborate nectar spoon distinguishes it from other species. Expert care; pure water and cool conditions non-negotiable.
